name: hig-components-menus
description: Comprehensive guidance for menu and button components following Apple's Human Interface Guidelines. Covers menus, context menus, dock menus, edit menus, the menu bar, toolbars, action buttons, pop-up buttons, pull-down buttons, disclosure controls, and standard buttons.
capabilities: Recommend appropriate menu types (context menus, dock menus, edit menus, menu bar) based on use case
Specify correct button styles and roles for different platforms (iOS, iPadOS, macOS, visionOS)
Design toolbar layouts with proper action prioritization and overflow behavior
Apply Apple's standard keyboard shortcuts and menu ordering conventions
Choose between pop-up buttons, pull-down buttons, and disclosure controls for specific scenarios
Implement platform-specific menu behaviors and accessibility requirements
author: sickn33
securitySummary: Static analysis detected 893 potential security issues across 11 files (1527 lines). All findings are false positives from a pure documentation skill containing Apple Human Interface Guidelines. Hardcoded URLs are legitimate documentation image references to developer.apple.com. 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' and 'C2 keyword' detections are triggered by common UI/UX terminology (button, control, push, etc.). 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' patterns are from markdown code blocks and inline code formatting. No executable code, no network operations, no external commands - read-only reference documentation.
valueStatement: Ensure your macOS and iOS interfaces follow Apple's design standards. This skill provides authoritative guidance on menu structure, button styles, and toolbar placement from Apple's Human Interface Guidelines.
limitations: Focuses exclusively on Apple platforms (iOS, iPadOS, macOS, visionOS, tvOS)
Provides design guidance only, not implementation code or API references
Content sourced from Apple's documentation may not cover latest beta features
Does not cover custom component designs that deviate from HIG standards

useCases.title: Design macOS Menu Structure
useCases.description: A developer building a macOS application needs to organize menu items, define keyboard shortcuts, and implement standard Edit menus. They use this skill to ensure their menu bar follows Apple's conventions and includes all expected standard items.

useCases.title: Choose Button Styles for iOS App
useCases.description: An iOS designer is creating a form with multiple action buttons and needs to determine which button styles to use. This skill helps them select appropriate button roles (primary, cancel, destructive) and ensure proper visual hierarchy.

useCases.title: Implement Context Menus
useCases.description: A cross-platform developer wants to add right-click context menus to their iPad and macOS app. They consult this skill to understand what actions belong in context menus versus primary UI, and how to ensure accessibility.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Menu Recommendation
promptTemplates.prompt: What type of menu should I use for [specific action/use case] on [platform]?
promptTemplates.scenario: User needs quick guidance on choosing the right menu component

promptTemplates.title: Button Style Selection
promptTemplates.prompt: I need buttons for [describe scenario - primary action, destructive action, etc.] on [platform]. Which button styles and roles should I use?
promptTemplates.scenario: User designing a view with multiple action buttons needs styling guidance

promptTemplates.title: Toolbar Design Review
promptTemplates.prompt: Review my toolbar design for [platform]. I have these actions: [list actions]. Are these the right choices for the toolbar versus menus?
promptTemplates.scenario: User validating toolbar layout and action placement

promptTemplates.title: Platform-Specific Behavior
promptTemplates.prompt: How should [menu/button component] behave differently on [platform 1] versus [platform 2]? What are the keyboard shortcuts and accessibility requirements?
promptTemplates.scenario: User building cross-platform app needs to account for platform differences

bestPractices: Every command must be reachable from the menu bar on macOS — toolbars and context menus supplement, not replace, menu items
Use system-defined button styles and roles rather than custom designs to maintain platform consistency and accessibility
Limit prominent buttons to one or two per view to reduce cognitive load and guide users to primary actions
antiPatterns: Putting commands only in context menus without also providing them in the menu bar or primary interface
Using multiple prominent button styles in the same view, which creates visual confusion about which action is preferred
Crowding toolbars with too many items — prioritize frequently used commands and move less common actions to overflow menus

faq.question: What is the difference between a pop-up button and a pull-down button?
faq.answer: Pop-up buttons are for selecting one option from a mutually exclusive set (like choosing a font size). They always display the current selection. Pull-down buttons are for action lists where no selection is currently active (like a 'New' button that offers different creation options).

faq.question: Should I use a primary button for a destructive action?
faq.answer: No. Never assign the primary role to a button that performs a destructive action, even if it is the most likely choice. The visual prominence of primary buttons can cause people to tap without reading. Use the destructive role instead, which uses system red to warn users.

faq.question: What is the minimum hit region size for buttons?
faq.answer: Buttons need a hit region of at least 44x44 points on iOS, iPadOS, and macOS. On visionOS, the minimum is 60x60 points. This ensures people can easily select buttons whether they use a fingertip, pointer, their eyes, or a remote.

faq.question: When should I use a context menu versus a toolbar?
faq.answer: Use toolbars for frequently used commands that act on the current view or selection. Use context menus for secondary or item-specific actions accessed via right-click or long-press. Never put a command only in a context menu — always provide a primary access path.

faq.question: What keyboard shortcuts should I use for standard Edit menu items?
faq.answer: Use Command+Z for Undo, Command+Shift+Z for Redo, Command+X for Cut, Command+C for Copy, Command+V for Paste, Command+A for Select All, and Command+F for Find. These are platform-standard shortcuts that users expect on macOS.

faq.question: Can I customize the appearance of system buttons?
faq.answer: System buttons offer built-in styles with customization options for color and size. You can tint buttons and choose from styles like borderless, bordered, or bordered prominent. However, avoid creating completely custom button appearances that deviate from system styles, as this reduces accessibility and platform consistency.
